title: Momentum & Newton's Second Law
subtitle: Newton's Second Law F=ma for Class 11 Physics (NCERT)

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
First, momentum is "how hard something is to stop" — it grows with both mass and speed. A slow truck and a fast cricket ball can both be hard to stop.
Newton's Second Law says: force is what changes momentum. The harder you push, the faster the momentum changes; the heavier the object, the less it speeds up for the same push.

blocks.heading: The Formula
blocks.body:
Momentum is mass times velocity:
Newton's Second Law — force is the rate of change of momentum, which for constant mass becomes:
Symbols:
\vec{p} — momentum (kg·m/s)
m — mass (kg)
\vec{v} — velocity (m/s)
\vec{a} — acceleration, how quickly velocity changes (m/s²)
\vec{F} — net force (N), where 1\,\text{N} = 1\,\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}^2

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body:
Push an empty shopping trolley and it shoots forward easily. Load it with rice bags and the same push barely moves it. Same force `F`, bigger mass `m`, so smaller acceleration `a`.
In cricket, a fast bowler gives the ball a large momentum (`p = mv`) with a big velocity — which is exactly why it's hard for the batsman to stop or redirect.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Roll a tennis ball and a heavier ball (or a filled water bottle) by flicking each with the same finger force. The light one accelerates much more. You've just felt `a = F / m` — for the same force, less mass means more acceleration.

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: If you could double the engine force of a scooter and the rider's mass at the same time, what happens to the acceleration? (Use `a = F / m` and see why it stays the same.)

quiz.question: The same force is applied to a 2 kg ball and a 6 kg ball. The 6 kg ball will have:
quiz.explanation: From a = F/m, acceleration is inversely proportional to mass. Triple the mass, one-third the acceleration for the same force.
